title: Measuring the Storm
subtitle: Volatility, VaR & stress tests — plainly

hook.metaphor: Before sailing, you check the forecast: how rough is it likely to get, and how bad could a freak storm be? Measuring market risk is exactly that forecasting — putting rough numbers on 'how much might we lose on a normal bad day' and 'how much could we lose in a once-in-a-decade gale'. You don't need the maths; you need to read the forecast and know its limits.
hook.scenario: A risk report proudly states: 'Our 1-day Value at Risk is ₹2 crore.' An executive relaxes — '₹2 crore is our worst case, then.' A risk officer winces. Why is the executive misreading the forecast?

learn: The basic gauge is **volatility** — how much a price bounces around. High volatility = a rougher sea = more risk. It's the raw measure of 'how choppy is this exposure?'

The headline number is **Value at Risk (VaR)**: an estimate of how much you might lose, with a given confidence, over a given period — e.g. '1-day VaR of ₹2 crore at 99%' means 'on 99% of normal days we shouldn't lose more than ₹2 crore.' Useful as a common yardstick — but widely *misread*.

Here's the trap our executive fell into: VaR is **not the worst case**. That 99% figure says nothing about the other 1% of days — and on those days the loss can be *far* bigger. VaR tells you the edge of the normal, not the size of the catastrophe beyond it. Treating VaR as 'the worst that can happen' is exactly how firms get blindsided.

Which is why VaR must be paired with **stress testing** and **scenario analysis**: deliberately asking 'what if rates spike 3%? what if this currency crashes 20%? what if 2008 happens again?' Stress tests probe the dangerous tail that VaR ignores.

And every measure rests on assumptions and history — so it carries **model risk** (Operational Risk again). Markets can do things they've never done before, breaking the model. The mature user treats these numbers as a useful, humble forecast, not a precise guarantee.
bridge: So at work, you can read a risk forecast without being fooled by it: *volatility* tells you how choppy, *VaR* tells you a normal-bad-day loss (NOT the worst case), and *stress tests* probe the rare catastrophe VaR ignores. When someone calls VaR 'the worst case', gently correct them — that misreading is how the 1% days sink boats.

interactions.title: Is VaR the worst case?
interactions.prompt: The exec hears '1-day VaR is ₹2 crore' and treats it as the worst possible loss. Why is that wrong?

options.label: It's right — VaR is the maximum you can lose.
options.feedback: This is the classic, dangerous misreading. VaR is a threshold for *normal* days; it deliberately says nothing about the worst 1%, where losses can be far larger.

options.label: VaR is a normal-day estimate; the rare tail beyond it can be far worse.
options.feedback: Exactly. '₹2cr at 99%' means most days stay under ₹2cr — but the other 1% of days are uncapped. You need stress tests to probe that tail.

options.label: VaR is meaningless, so ignore it.
options.feedback: Too far — VaR is a useful common yardstick for normal conditions. The fix isn't to discard it, but to understand its limit and pair it with stress testing.

interactions.title: Risk-measurement toolkit
interactions.prompt: Match each tool to what it tells you.

pairs.right: How much a price bounces around
A likely loss on a normal bad day (not the worst case)
Loss under a specific severe scenario
Exploring 'what if X extreme thing happens?'
The chance your measure itself is wrong

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “You forecast market risk with volatility (how choppy), VaR (a normal-bad-day loss — not the worst case), and stress tests (the rare gale VaR ignores) — and you stay humble because the model can always be wrong.”

checkpoint.q: A '1-day VaR of ₹2 crore at 99%' means…
options: You can never lose more than ₹2 crore
On ~99% of normal days, losses shouldn't exceed ₹2 crore
checkpoint.explain: It's a normal-day threshold; the worst 1% of days can be far worse — VaR isn't the worst case.

checkpoint.q: Why pair VaR with stress testing?
options: Because stress tests probe the severe tail that VaR ignores
checkpoint.explain: Stress tests ask 'what if something extreme happens?' — exactly the catastrophe VaR is silent on.

options: How much a price bounces around (its choppiness)
checkpoint.explain: Higher volatility means a rougher sea and more market risk.
